category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Ordinary Differential Equations/Second Order ODEs

info.text: CSIR NET Mathematics â€” ODEs: Second Order ODEs. MCQ, 20 questions covering constant coefficients, Cauchy-Euler, method of undetermined coefficients, variation of parameters, power series, Wronskian, and oscillation theory.

name.text: ODE38-Q01 Characteristic equation â€” constant coefficients

questiontext.text: Find the general solution of \(y'' - 5y' + 6y = 0\).

feedback.text: Characteristic equation: \(r^2-5r+6=0\), factoring: \((r-2)(r-3)=0\), roots \(r=2,3\). General solution: \(y=C_1e^{2x}+C_2e^{3x}\). The Wronskian \(W(e^{2x},e^{3x})=3e^{5x}-2e^{5x}=e^{5x}\neq 0\), confirming linear independence.
Incorrect. The roots of \(r^2-5r+6=0\) are \(r=2\) and \(r=3\) (both positive).
Incorrect. This is the form for a repeated root; \(r^2-5r+6=(r-2)(r-3)\) has two distinct roots.
Incorrect. Complex roots arise when the discriminant is negative; here \(\Delta=25-24=1>0\), giving real roots.

name.text: ODE38-Q02 Complex roots â€” underdamped oscillation

questiontext.text: Find the general solution of \(y'' + 4y' + 13y = 0\).

feedback.text: Characteristic equation: \(r^2+4r+13=0\). Discriminant: \(16-52=-36<0\). Roots: \(r=\frac{-4\pm\sqrt{-36}}{2}=-2\pm 3i\). General solution for complex roots \(\alpha\pm\beta i\): \(y=e^{\alpha x}(C_1\cos\beta x+C_2\sin\beta x)\). Here \(\alpha=-2,\beta=3\), so \(y=e^{-2x}(C_1\cos 3x+C_2\sin 3x)\).
Incorrect. The real part of the roots is \(-2\) (negative, damping), not \(+2\).
Incorrect. Hyperbolic functions arise for real roots; complex roots give trigonometric functions.
Incorrect. While formally correct, real solutions use the real basis \(e^{-2x}\cos 3x\) and \(e^{-2x}\sin 3x\).

Characteristic equation: \(r^2-6r+9=(r-3)^2=0\). Repeated root \(r=3\). For repeated root \(r_0\), general solution is \(y=(C_1+C_2 x)e^{r_0 x}\). The Wronskian of \(e^{3x}\) and \(xe^{3x}\) is \(e^{6x}\neq 0\), confirming independence. The repeated root arises from critical damping in mechanical systems.
Incorrect. The roots are both \(+3\) (not \(\pm 3\)); for \(r^2-6r+9=(r-3)^2=0\).
Incorrect. For a double root, the two solutions are \(e^{3x}\) and \(xe^{3x}\) (not \(x^2e^{3x}\)).
Incorrect. Complex root form applies when roots are complex; here the double root \(r=3\) is real.

questiontext.text: Find a particular solution of \(y'' + 4y = \sin 2x\) (resonance case).

feedback.text: The homogeneous solution is \(y_h=C_1\cos 2x+C_2\sin 2x\). Since \(\sin 2x\) is part of \(y_h\) (resonance), we multiply by \(x\): try \(y_p=x(A\cos 2x+B\sin 2x)\). Computing \(y_p''\) and substituting into \(y_p''+4y_p=\sin 2x\): \(y_p'=A\cos 2x+B\sin 2x+x(-2A\sin 2x+2B\cos 2x)\); \(y_p''=-4A\sin 2x+4B\cos 2x+x(-4A\cos 2x-4B\sin 2x)-2A\sin 2x+2B\cos 2x+2B\cos 2x... \)After full calculation: \(-4A\sin 2x+4B\cos 2x=\sin 2x\), giving \(A=-1/4, B=0\). So \(y_p=-\frac{x\cos 2x}{4}\).
Incorrect. Since \(\sin 2x\) is a homogeneous solution (resonance), the simple trial function fails; multiply by \(x\).
Incorrect. For resonance, the correct form is \(y_p=x(A\cos 2x+B\sin 2x)\).
Incorrect. While the form is partially correct (multiply by \(x\)), the coefficient works out to \(A=-1/4\) and \(B=0\).

questiontext.text: Using variation of parameters, find a particular solution of \(y'' + y = \sec x\).

feedback.text: Homogeneous: \(y_h=C_1\cos x+C_2\sin x\). Wronskian: \(W=\cos x\cdot\cos x-\sin x\cdot(-\sin x)=1\). Variation of parameters: \(u_1'=-\frac{y_2 g}{W}=-\sin x\cdot\sec x=-\tan x\), so \(u_1=\ln|\cos x|\). \(u_2'=\frac{y_1 g}{W}=\cos x\cdot\sec x=1\), so \(u_2=x\). Thus \(y_p=u_1\cos x+u_2\sin x=\cos x\ln|\cos x|+x\sin x\).
Incorrect. The variation of parameters formula gives \(y_p=u_1\cos x+u_2\sin x\); both terms contribute.
Incorrect. Check: \((x\cos x)''+(x\cos x)=(-2\sin x-x\cos x)+(x\cos x)=-2\sin x\neq\sec x\).
Incorrect. The correct formula gives \(\ln|\cos x|\) (from \(u_1=\int(-\tan x)dx=\ln|\cos x|\)), multiplied by \(\cos x\).

questiontext.text: Find the general solution of the Cauchy-Euler equation \(x^2 y'' + 2xy' - 6y = 0\).

feedback.text: Substitute \(y=x^m\): \(m(m-1)+2m-6=0\), i.e., \(m^2+m-6=0\), factoring: \((m+3)(m-2)=0\), roots \(m=2,-3\). General solution: \(y=C_1x^2+C_2x^{-3}\). Check: for \(y=x^2\): \(x^2\cdot 2+2x\cdot 2x-6x^2=2x^2+4x^2-6x^2=0\). âś“
Incorrect. The characteristic equation \(m^2+m-6=0\) has roots \(m=2\) and \(m=-3\), not \(m=3\) and \(m=-2\).
Incorrect. Cauchy-Euler solutions are power functions \(x^m\), not exponentials.
Incorrect. The logarithmic form appears for repeated roots; here the roots are distinct (\(m=2,-3\)).

questiontext.text: The Wronskian of \(y_1=e^x\sin x\) and \(y_2=e^x\cos x\) is:

feedback.text: \(W(y_1,y_2)=y_1y_2'-y_2y_1'\). \(y_1'=e^x(\sin x+\cos x)\), \(y_2'=e^x(\cos x-\sin x)\). \(W=e^x\sin x\cdot e^x(\cos x-\sin x)-e^x\cos x\cdot e^x(\sin x+\cos x)\) \(=e^{2x}[\sin x\cos x-\sin^2 x-\cos x\sin x-\cos^2 x]=e^{2x}[-\sin^2 x-\cos^2 x]=-e^{2x}\). Since \(W\neq 0\), the functions are linearly independent.
Incorrect. The Wronskian is \(-e^{2x}\) (note the sign).
Incorrect. \(W=0\) would imply linear dependence; but \(e^x\sin x\) and \(e^x\cos x\) are linearly independent.
Incorrect. Careful computation gives \(W=-e^{2x}\).

questiontext.text: Abel's theorem states that the Wronskian of two solutions of \(y'' + p(x)y' + q(x)y=0\) satisfies:

feedback.text: Abel's identity: differentiating \(W=y_1y_2'-y_2y_1'\): \(W'=y_1y_2''-y_2y_1''\). Using the ODE: \(y_i''=-py_i'-qy_i\). Substituting: \(W'=y_1(-py_2'-qy_2)-y_2(-py_1'-qy_1)=-p(y_1y_2'-y_2y_1')=-pW\). Thus \(W'+pW=0\), giving \(W=Ce^{-\int p\,dx}\). Consequence: \(W\) is either identically 0 (linearly dependent) or never 0 (linearly independent) on an interval where \(p\) is continuous.
Incorrect. The sign: \(W'+pW=0\) gives \(W=Ce^{-\int p\,dx}\) (negative sign in the exponent).
Incorrect. \(W\) is constant only when \(p(x)=0\); in general, \(W=Ce^{-\int p\,dx}\).
Incorrect. The Wronskian depends on \(p(x)\), not \(q(x)\).

name.text: ODE38-Q09 Power series solution â€” ordinary point

questiontext.text: For the ODE \(y'' - xy' - y = 0\), \(x=0\) is an ordinary point. The power series solution \(y=\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n x^n\) gives the recurrence:

feedback.text: Substituting \(y=\sum a_nx^n\): \(y''=\sum_{n=2}n(n-1)a_nx^{n-2}=\sum_{n=0}(n+2)(n+1)a_{n+2}x^n\). \(xy'=\sum na_nx^n\). \(y=\sum a_nx^n\). The ODE becomes: \(\sum_{n=0}[(n+2)(n+1)a_{n+2}-na_n-a_n]x^n=0\). So \((n+2)(n+1)a_{n+2}=(n+1)a_n\), giving \(a_{n+2}=\frac{a_n}{n+2}\). Two families: even-index from \(a_0\) and odd-index from \(a_1\).
Incorrect. The recurrence from \(y''-xy'-y=0\) gives \((n+2)(n+1)a_{n+2}=(n+1)a_n\), so \(a_{n+2}=a_n/(n+2)\).
Incorrect. The sign is positive: \((n+2)(n+1)a_{n+2}=(n+1)a_n>0\) (for \(n\geq 0\)).
Incorrect. The recurrence relates \(a_{n+2}\) to \(a_n\) (skipping one index), not \(a_{n+1}\).

name.text: ODE38-Q10 Frobenius method â€” regular singular point

questiontext.text: For the Bessel equation of order zero: \(xy'' + y' + xy = 0\), what is the indicial equation at the regular singular point \(x=0\)?

feedback.text: Writing in standard Frobenius form: \(y''+(1/x)y'+y=0\). Here \(p(x)=1/x\) and \(q(x)=1\). \(x\to 0\): \(p_0=\lim_{x\to 0}xp(x)=1\), \(q_0=\lim_{x\to 0}x^2q(x)=0\). Indicial equation: \(r(r-1)+p_0 r+q_0=0\), i.e., \(r(r-1)+r=r^2=0\). Double root \(r=0\). The two solutions are \(J_0(x)\) (Bessel function) and \(Y_0(x)\) (Neumann function, contains \(\ln x\)).
Incorrect. The indicial equation uses \(p_0=1\) and \(q_0=0\): \(r^2-r+r+0=r^2=0\).
Incorrect. The indicial equation for Bessel order 0 is \(r^2=0\), not \(r(r+1)=0\).
Incorrect. The indicial equation is \(r^2=0\); the \(-r\) term cancels with \(p_0 r=r\).

questiontext.text: The Green's function \(G(x,t)\) for the BVP \(-y''=f(x)\), \(y(0)=y(1)=0\) is:

answer.text: \(G(x,t) = \begin{cases} t(1-x) & 0\leq t\leq x\leq 1 \\ x(1-t) & 0\leq x\leq t\leq 1 \end{cases}\)

feedback.text: For \(-y''=f\) with \(y(0)=y(1)=0\): Homogeneous solutions \(y_1=x\) (satisfies \(y(0)=0\)) and \(y_2=1-x\) (satisfies \(y(1)=0\)). Wronskian: \(W(y_1,y_2)=x(-1)-(1-x)(1)=-1\). Green's function: for \(t<x\): \(G=y_1(t)y_2(x)/(-W\cdot(-1))=t(1-x)\); for \(t>x\): \(G=y_1(x)y_2(t)=x(1-t)\). The solution is \(y(x)=\int_0^1 G(x,t)f(t)\,dt\).
Incorrect. The Green's function is piecewise defined with different expressions for \(x\leq t\) and \(x\geq t\).
Incorrect. This would be the kernel for eigenfunction expansions, not the Green's function directly.
Incorrect. The Dirichlet boundary conditions modify the form from the full-line Green's function.

name.text: ODE38-Q12 Oscillation theorem â€” Sturm comparison

questiontext.text: Sturm's comparison theorem: if \(q_1(x)>q_2(x)\) on \((a,b)\) and \(u,v\) solve \(u''+q_1(x)u=0\) and \(v''+q_2(x)v=0\) respectively, then between any two consecutive zeros of \(v\) there is:

feedback.text: Sturm's comparison theorem: if \(q_1>q_2\) on \((a,b)\), the solutions of the equation with larger coefficient \(q_1\) oscillate at least as rapidly as those with \(q_2\). More precisely: between any two consecutive zeros of \(v\), there is at least one zero of \(u\). Application: comparing \(y''+y=0\) (solutions: \(\sin x\)) with \(y''+\frac{1}{4}y=0\) (solutions: \(\sin(x/2)\)) â€” \(\sin x\) zeros are denser than \(\sin(x/2)\) zeros, consistent with \(1>1/4\).
Incorrect. The opposite is true: larger coefficient means faster oscillation, so more zeros.
Incorrect. Sturm guarantees at least one, not exactly one.
Incorrect. The theorem guarantees at least one zero; there could be more.

name.text: ODE38-Q13 Undetermined coefficients â€” polynomial RHS

questiontext.text: Find a particular solution of \(y'' - 3y' + 2y = 3x^2\).

feedback.text: Try \(y_p=Ax^2+Bx+C\). Then \(y_p''=2A\), \(y_p'=2Ax+B\). Substituting: \(2A-3(2Ax+B)+2(Ax^2+Bx+C)=3x^2\). Matching coefficients: \(x^2\): \(2A=3\Rightarrow A=3/2\). \(x^1\): \(-6A+2B=0\Rightarrow B=3A=9/2\). \(x^0\): \(2A-3B+2C=0\Rightarrow 3-27/2+2C=0\Rightarrow 2C=21/2\Rightarrow C=21/4\). So \(y_p=\frac{3}{2}x^2+\frac{9}{2}x+\frac{21}{4}\).
Incorrect. Matching lower-degree terms is necessary; the complete particular solution has \(x\) and constant terms.
Incorrect. The particular solution should match the degree of the right side (degree 2).
Incorrect. The coefficients from matching give \(A=3/2\), \(B=9/2\), \(C=21/4\).

name.text: ODE38-Q14 Boundary value problem â€” eigenvalues

questiontext.text: The eigenvalue problem \(y'' + \lambda y = 0\), \(y(0)=0\), \(y(\pi)=0\) has eigenvalues:

feedback.text: For \(\lambda>0\): general solution \(y=A\cos(\sqrt\lambda x)+B\sin(\sqrt\lambda x)\). \(y(0)=0\Rightarrow A=0\). \(y(\pi)=0\Rightarrow B\sin(\sqrt\lambda\pi)=0\). For nontrivial solution: \(\sqrt\lambda\pi=n\pi\), so \(\lambda=n^2\), \(n=1,2,3,\ldots\) For \(\lambda\leq 0\): only trivial solution. Eigenfunctions: \(y_n=\sin(nx)\), forming an orthonormal basis for \(L^2(0,\pi)\). This is the foundation of Fourier sine series.
Incorrect. The interval is \([0,\pi]\), so \(\sqrt\lambda\pi=n\pi\) gives \(\lambda=n^2\) (not \(n^2\pi^2\)).
Incorrect. The eigenvalues are perfect squares: \(\lambda_n=n^2\).
Incorrect. Only \(\lambda=n^2\) for positive integers \(n\) give nontrivial solutions satisfying both boundary conditions.

questiontext.text: The Airy equation \(y'' - xy = 0\) has \(x=0\) as an ordinary point. The two linearly independent power series solutions can be expressed in terms of Airy functions \(Ai(x)\) and \(Bi(x)\). The recurrence relation for coefficients \(a_n\) in \(y=\sum a_n x^n\) is:

feedback.text: Substituting \(y=\sum a_nx^n\) into \(y''=xy\): \(\sum_{n=2}n(n-1)a_nx^{n-2}=x\sum a_nx^n=\sum a_nx^{n+1}\). Reindexing: \(\sum_{n=0}(n+2)(n+1)a_{n+2}x^n=\sum_{n=0}a_{n-1}x^n\) (with \(a_{-1}=0\)). Matching \(n\geq 1\): \((n+2)(n+1)a_{n+2}=a_{n-1}\), i.e., \(a_{n+3}=\frac{a_n}{(n+3)(n+2)}\) (shifting \(n\to n+1\)). This gives solutions from \(a_0\) (with \(a_1=a_2=0\)) and from \(a_1\) (with \(a_0=a_2=0\)).
Incorrect. While equivalent (just different indexing), the standard form using \(a_{n+3}=\frac{a_n}{(n+3)(n+2)}\) is more transparent.
Incorrect. This does not correctly capture the skip-2 recurrence of the Airy equation.
Incorrect. This recurrence (for the exponential function) does not apply here; the Airy equation gives a 3-step recurrence.

name.text: ODE38-Q16 Variation of parameters for second-order

questiontext.text: For \(y'' + p(x)y' + q(x)y = g(x)\) with fundamental solutions \(y_1, y_2\), the variation of parameters formulas for \(u_1', u_2'\) (where \(y_p=u_1y_1+u_2y_2\)) are:

feedback.text: The system for \(u_1', u_2'\) is: \(u_1'y_1+u_2'y_2=0\) and \(u_1'y_1'+u_2'y_2'=g\). By Cramer's rule: \(u_1'=\frac{-y_2 g}{W}\) and \(u_2'=\frac{y_1 g}{W}\) where \(W=y_1y_2'-y_2y_1'\neq 0\). Then \(y_p=u_1y_1+u_2y_2=-y_1\int\frac{y_2 g}{W}dx+y_2\int\frac{y_1 g}{W}dx\). This method works for any continuous \(g\), unlike undetermined coefficients.
Incorrect. The signs are swapped; Cramer's rule gives \(u_1'=-y_2g/W\) and \(u_2'=y_1g/W\).
Incorrect. The formula requires the other solution in the numerator (cross terms), with alternating signs.
Incorrect. The two variations have different numerators \(y_2\) and \(y_1\) respectively.

questiontext.text: For the damped oscillator \(my'' + cy' + ky = 0\) with \(m=1, c=4, k=3\), the system is classified as:

answer.text: Overdamped (characteristic roots are real, negative, and distinct)

feedback.text: Characteristic equation: \(r^2+4r+3=0\), discriminant \(\Delta=16-12=4>0\). Roots: \(r=(-4\pm 2)/2=-1\) and \(-3\). Both real and negative. General solution: \(y=C_1e^{-t}+C_2e^{-3t}\). The system is overdamped: \(c^2>4mk\) (i.e., \(16>12\)). Solution decays without oscillation. Critical damping: \(c^2=4mk\). Underdamping: \(c^2<4mk\).

answer.text: Underdamped (roots are complex with negative real part)

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(\Delta=16-12=4>0\), so roots are real. Underdamped requires \(\Delta<0\).
Incorrect. Critical damping requires \(\Delta=0\), i.e., \(c^2=4mk=12\). Here \(c^2=16\neq 12\).
Incorrect. Both roots \(-1\) and \(-3\) are negative, so the system is stable and decays.

questiontext.text: Find a particular solution of \(x^2 y'' - 2y = x^3\) using variation of parameters (homogeneous solutions are \(y_1=x^2\) and \(y_2=x^{-1}\)).

feedback.text: Rewrite as \(y''-2x^{-2}y=x\). Wronskian: \(W(x^2,x^{-1})=x^2\cdot(-x^{-2})-x^{-1}\cdot 2x=-1-2=-3\). Variation of parameters with \(g(x)=x\): \(u_1'=\frac{-y_2\cdot g}{W}=\frac{-x^{-1}\cdot x}{-3}=\frac{1}{3}\), so \(u_1=x/3\). \(u_2'=\frac{y_1\cdot g}{W}=\frac{x^2\cdot x}{-3}=-x^3/3\), so \(u_2=-x^4/12\). Thus \(y_p=\frac{x}{3}\cdot x^2+(-\frac{x^4}{12})\cdot x^{-1}=\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^3}{12}=\frac{x^3}{4}\).
Incorrect. Check: \(x^2(6x)-2x^3=6x^3-2x^3=4x^3\neq x^3\).
Incorrect. The variation of parameters calculation gives \(y_p=x^3/4\).
Incorrect. Logarithmic terms appear for Cauchy-Euler with repeated roots; here the homogeneous solutions are \(x^2\) and \(x^{-1}\).

questiontext.text: The Sturm-Liouville operator \(Ly = -(py')' + qy\) on \([a,b]\) with appropriate boundary conditions is self-adjoint. Its eigenvalues have which property?

answer.text: All eigenvalues are real, and eigenfunctions corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal with respect to the weight function \(w(x)\)

feedback.text: For the regular Sturm-Liouville problem \(-(py')'+qy=\lambda wy\) with \(p>0\) and appropriate self-adjoint boundary conditions: (1) All eigenvalues \(\lambda_n\) are real. (2) Eigenvalues form a discrete sequence \(\lambda_1<\lambda_2<\cdots\to\infty\). (3) Eigenfunctions \(\phi_n\) are orthogonal: \(\int_a^b w\phi_m\phi_n\,dx=0\) for \(m\neq n\). (4) Eigenfunctions form a complete basis for \(L^2_w(a,b)\). This unifies Fourier series, Legendre polynomials, Bessel functions, etc.
Incorrect. Self-adjoint operators on real Hilbert spaces have only real eigenvalues.
Incorrect. Regular Sturm-Liouville problems have infinitely many eigenvalues accumulating at \(+\infty\).
Incorrect. Eigenfunctions are generally oscillatory; only the zero eigenfunction (if it exists) could be constant.

questiontext.text: For a second-order linear ODE \(y'' + p(x)y' + q(x)y = g(x)\), the general solution is:

answer.text: \(y = y_h + y_p\), where \(y_h = C_1y_1 + C_2y_2\) is the general homogeneous solution and \(y_p\) is any particular solution

feedback.text: The superposition principle: for a linear ODE, the general solution of the non-homogeneous equation is the sum of the general solution of the homogeneous equation (with two arbitrary constants \(C_1, C_2\)) plus any particular solution of the non-homogeneous equation. This follows from linearity: if \(y_p\) satisfies \(Ly_p=g\) and \(y_h\) satisfies \(Ly_h=0\), then \(L(y_h+y_p)=0+g=g\). All solutions have this form.
Incorrect. Without the homogeneous component, we cannot satisfy general initial conditions.
Incorrect. Scaling a particular solution generally doesn't give another solution to the non-homogeneous equation.

answer.text: \(y = y_1 y_2 + y_p\) (product of homogeneous solutions plus particular)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The product of two solutions is generally not a solution. The homogeneous part is a linear combination \(C_1y_1+C_2y_2\).
